column1: # Principle: Types are first-class. Type literals push as values; the
# `type`/`untype`/`typeof` words install, retract, and inspect type
# bindings. None is the explicit unit type, spelled `null`.
# This spec consolidates everything about types in aqleng:
#   1. Type-name words push type-literal values (Integer, String, …).
#   2. `null` parses as a None type literal.
#   3. `type NAME body` installs a type binding (NAME starts [A-Z]).
#   5. `typeof v` returns an Atom naming v's type (or its metatype if
#   6. Type literals dispatch through normal sigs with one quirk: the
#      Data==nil rejection in match.go ONLY rejects type literals for
#      TMap and TList — scalar leaf types (Integer / Decimal / String /
#      Boolean) are silently accepted by sigs declaring them. The
#      handler then reads the zero value from AsX(), which is a real
#   7. None matches no concrete-typed sig; only Any-typed sigs accept it.
# Type literals are the language's value-level handle on its types —
# they're how you spell "the type Integer" or "the type List". Every
# reflective / generic operation (predicates, type equality, type
# unions, dispatch) builds on them. The shadow / pop semantics of the
# type stack mirror the def stack so a single mental model covers both.
# This is the aqleng (engine-kernel) subset. The richer type words
# (record, table, object, make, is, guard, inspect, base, convert, …)
# are layered on top in production aql; their semantics depend on
# parser / native-mod features that are intentionally out of aqleng
#   addq     — [Number Number] forward-prec — args[1] + args[0] (decimal contagion)
#   subq     — [Number Number] forward-prec — args[1] - args[0] (decimal contagion)
#   concatq  — [String String] forward-prec — args[1] + args[0] (string concat)
#   firstq   — [List] forward-prec — first element or None for empty list
#   lengthq  — [List] forward-prec — list length as Integer

column3: note

# previous binding when the top is popped.

column1: # 9. Dispatch — type literals are rejected at concrete-payload sigs
# A type literal (Data == nil) is rejected by every sig slot whose
# expected type is a concrete payload type. This includes the scalar
# leaves (Integer / Decimal / String / Boolean), TNumber, TList, TMap,
# TAtom, etc. The matcher (aqleng/go/match.go via rejectsTypeLiteral
# in signature.go) flags Data == nil as inadmissible there even if
# A type literal IS still legitimately accepted at:
#   - TAny slots — universal catch-all; the handler is expected to
#     handle both concrete payloads and type literals.
#     etc. consume a type literal as the type itself.
#   - The TNone slot, when the value is `null` — None has a single
# This eliminates the old footgun where `addq Integer 1` would
# silently compute `addq 0 1`. Now the [TNumber, TNumber] sig of
# addq rejects the type literal and the call falls through to no
# the [Integer] sig is now off-limits to type literals, every type-
# literal call falls through to [Any] uniformly.

column3: Integer type literal rejected by [Integer]; falls to [Any]
Number is the parent of Integer; doesn't match [Integer] anyway; falls to [Any]
Scalar is broader than Integer; falls to [Any]
Decimal type literal rejected if a [Decimal] sig existed; falls to [Any]
no [String] overload; falls to [Any]
no [Boolean] overload; falls to [Any]
Any type literal hits the [Any] catch-all
null at [Any] — None values pass through
